statement.markdown:
Quick Sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that picks an element as a pivot and partitions the given array around the picked pivot. The goal is to sort the array in ascending order.

Given an array of integers, implement the Quick Sort algorithm to sort the array in ascending order.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer N, the number of elements in the array.
The second line contains N integers, the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The sorted array in ascending order, with each element on a new line.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array.

examples.explanation: The Quick Sort algorithm sorts the array in ascending order.
The Quick Sort algorithm handles duplicate elements.
The Quick Sort algorithm handles a single-element array.
The Quick Sort algorithm handles negative numbers and large ranges.

hints.text: The Quick Sort algorithm starts by selecting a 'pivot' element from the array. This pivot will serve as a reference point to partition the array.

The array is partitioned into two parts: elements less than the pivot and elements greater than the pivot. This process is recursive, meaning it is applied to the sub-arrays on either side of the pivot.

A key part of the Quick Sort algorithm is the partitioning process. This involves rearranging the array such that all elements less than the pivot come before it, and all elements greater than the pivot come after it.

The base case for the recursion is when the sub-array has one or zero elements, in which case it is already sorted. The challenge is in implementing the partitioning step efficiently and handling the recursive calls.

The technique at the core of Quick Sort is the divide-and-conquer approach, where the problem is broken down into smaller sub-problems (sorting sub-arrays), and the solutions to these sub-problems are combined to solve the original problem.

editorial.title: Quick Sort Analysis
editorial.intuition: The problem requires sorting an array of integers, and the quick sort algorithm is a suitable approach. The intuition behind quick sort is to select a pivot element, partition the array around it, and recursively sort the subarrays.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to use a simple sorting algorithm like bubble sort or insertion sort, which have a time complexity of O(n^2). These algorithms are easy to understand and implement but are inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The quick sort algorithm is a divide-and-conquer approach that selects a pivot element, partitions the array around it, and recursively sorts the subarrays. This approach has a time complexity of O(n log n) on average.
editorial.optimalSolution: The provided reference solution implements the quick sort algorithm using list comprehensions to create the left, middle, and right partitions. This approach has a time complexity of O(n log n) and a space complexity of O(n), making it efficient for large inputs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the quick sort algorithm can be proven by induction. The base case is when the array has one or zero elements, which is already sorted. For the inductive step, assume that the algorithm correctly sorts arrays of size k or less. For an array of size k+1, the algorithm selects a pivot, partitions the array, and recursively sorts the subarrays. By the inductive hypothesis, the subarrays are correctly sorted, and the final concatenation of the subarrays and the pivot element results in a sorted array.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm with an example input, such as [3, 6, 8, 10, 1, 2, 1], helps to illustrate the partitioning and recursive sorting steps.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly selecting the pivot element, which can lead to poor performance.
Not handling duplicate elements correctly, which can result in incorrect sorting.
Not using a efficient partitioning scheme, which can increase the time complexity.
Not considering the edge case where the input array is already sorted.
Not using a recursive approach, which can lead to stack overflow errors for large inputs.
Not optimizing the algorithm for space complexity, which can result in excessive memory usage.
